questions.question:
A CPU has a five-stage pipeline and runs at 1 GHz frequency. Instruction fetch happens in the first stage of the pipeline. A conditional branch instruction computes the target address and evaluates the condition in the third stage of the pipeline. The processor stops fetching new instructions following a conditional branch until the branch outcome is known. A program executes 10^9 instructions out of which 20% are conditional branches. If each instruction takes one clock cycle and a conditional branch causes a 2-cycle stall, what is the total execution time of the program?
A. 1.0 seconds
B. 1.2 seconds
C. 1.4 seconds
D. 1.6 seconds
questions.to_find: Total execution time of the program given 20% conditional branches each causing 2-cycle stall

understand.plain: A 5-stage pipeline runs at 1 GHz. When a conditional branch is encountered, the processor stalls for 2 cycles because the branch outcome is only known after stage 3. With 20% of instructions being conditional branches, we need to calculate the effective CPI and total execution time.

keywords.explain: The number of extra stall cycles inserted into the pipeline when a branch is encountered and the outcome is not yet known.
keywords.example: If branch is resolved in stage 3 and fetch is in stage 1, stall = 3 - 1 = 2 cycles.

keywords.explain: Average number of clock cycles needed to execute one instruction. For a stall-free pipeline CPI = 1; hazards increase this.
Inserting idle (bubble) cycles into the pipeline to wait for a required value or decision.
keywords.example: After a branch fetch, 2 bubbles are inserted until the condition is evaluated in stage 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='520' height='160' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='20' y='28' class='lbl ink'>Branch stall: 2 cycles</text><rect x='20' y='40' width='80' height='34' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Branch</text><rect x='110' y='40' width='80' height='34' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' rx='4'/><text x='150' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>Stall</text><rect x='200' y='40' width='80' height='34' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' rx='4'/><text x='240' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>Stall</text><rect x='290' y='40' width='80' height='34' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Next I</text><text x='20' y='118' class='sub mut'>CPI = 1 + 0.20 x 2 = 1.4</text><text x='20' y='140' class='sub mut'>Time = 10^9 x 1.4 / (1x10^9) = 1.4 seconds</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Pipeline diagram showing a branch instruction followed by 2 stall cycles before the next instruction can be fetched.

given.aim: Compute total execution time given branch stall cycles and branch frequency

terms.connects: Multiplied by CPI and divided by clock frequency to get time

terms.meaning: 20% = 0.20 of all instructions are conditional branches
terms.example: 2 out of every 10 instructions cause a stall
terms.connects: Scales the branch penalty contribution to CPI

terms.meaning: 2 stall cycles per conditional branch (branch resolved in stage 3, fetch is stage 1, so 3-1=2 stalls)
terms.example: After fetching a branch, the pipeline inserts 2 bubbles
terms.connects: Multiplied by branch fraction to get extra CPI

given.plan: Calculate effective CPI using CPI = 1 + (branch_fraction * branch_penalty). Then multiply by total instructions and divide by clock frequency to get execution time.

steps.title: Determine branch penalty
steps.apply: Branch is resolved in stage 3, fetch is stage 1. Penalty = 3 - 1 = 2 stall cycles.
steps.note: The processor stops fetching until stage 3 completes, meaning stages 2 and 3 of the branch instruction are wasted fetch slots.

steps.title: Calculate effective CPI
steps.note: The base CPI of 1 (ideal pipeline) is increased by the weighted penalty.

steps.title: Calculate total execution time
steps.note: The 10^9 factors cancel, leaving execution time = CPI in seconds for this problem.

solution.result: Total execution time = 1.4 seconds. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Effective CPI formula: CPI = 1 + branch_fraction * branch_penalty. Total time = N * CPI / clock_frequency.

questions.question:
Consider a pipelined processor with the following four stages:
IF: Instruction Fetch
ID: Instruction Decode and Operand Fetch
EX: Execute
WB: Write Back
The IF, ID and WB stages take one clock cycle each to complete the operation. The number of clock cycles for the EX stage depends on the instruction. The ADD and SUB instructions need 1 clock cycle and the MUL instruction needs 3 clock cycles in the EX stage. Operand forwarding is used in the pipelined processor. What is the number of clock cycles taken to complete the following sequence of instructions?

ADD  R2, R1, R0    R2 <- R1+R0
MUL  R4, R3, R2    R4 <- R3*R2
SUB  R6, R5, R4    R6 <- R5-R4

A. 7
B. 8
C. 10
D. 14
questions.to_find: Total clock cycles to execute the three-instruction sequence with operand forwarding

understand.plain: Three instructions (ADD, MUL, SUB) are executed in a 4-stage pipeline where MUL takes 3 EX cycles. Each instruction depends on the previous one (RAW dependency). With operand forwarding, a result can be forwarded from the end of EX to the beginning of the next instruction's EX stage, reducing stalls.

keywords.explain: A hardware technique that routes computed values directly from one pipeline stage output to another stage's input without waiting for WB.
keywords.example: ADD result in EX can be forwarded to MUL's EX input directly.

keywords.explain: A data hazard where a later instruction needs a value produced by an earlier instruction that hasn't been written back yet.
keywords.example: MUL reads R2 which ADD writes; if ADD hasn't finished EX, MUL must stall or use forwarding.

keywords.explain: Some instructions (like MUL) need more than one clock cycle in the EX stage.
keywords.example: MUL takes 3 EX cycles, so its result is not available until 2 extra cycles after a 1-cycle instruction.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 560 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='560' height='200' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='10' y='22' class='sub ink'>Cycle:  1    2    3    4    5    6    7    8</text><text x='10' y='50' class='sub ink'>ADD:   IF   ID   EX   WB</text><text x='10' y='80' class='sub ink'>MUL:        IF   ID   EX1  EX2  EX3  WB</text><text x='10' y='110' class='sub ink'>SUB:             IF   ID   --   --   EX   WB</text><text x='10' y='145' class='sub mut'>MUL reads R2 forwarded from ADD EX (end of cycle 3).</text><text x='10' y='165' class='sub mut'>SUB reads R4 forwarded from MUL EX3 (end of cycle 6).</text><text x='10' y='185' class='sub mut'>Total cycles = 8</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Pipeline execution diagram: ADD executes in cycles 1-4, MUL in cycles 2-7 (3 EX cycles), SUB in cycles 3-8. Total = 8 cycles.

given.aim: Find the total number of clock cycles for the three instruction sequence with forwarding

terms.connects: R2 is forwarded to MUL at end of cycle 3

terms.meaning: Instruction 2: 3 EX cycles. Reads R2 (from ADD), writes R4.
terms.example: With forwarding: IF(2) ID(3) EX1(4) EX2(5) EX3(6) WB(7)
terms.connects: R4 is forwarded to SUB at end of cycle 6

terms.meaning: Instruction 3: 1 EX cycle. Reads R4 (from MUL), writes R6.
terms.example: Must wait for R4: IF(3) ID(4) stall(5) stall(6) EX(7) WB(8)

terms.meaning: Result forwarded from end of EX to start of next instruction's EX
terms.example: MUL can start EX at cycle 4 because R2 is ready at end of cycle 3
terms.connects: Eliminates all stalls for ADD->MUL dependency; reduces but cannot eliminate stalls for MUL->SUB

given.plan: Trace each instruction through the pipeline cycle by cycle. Apply forwarding: a result is available at the end of the last EX cycle and can be forwarded to the beginning of the dependent instruction's EX stage. Count total cycles until the last WB completes.

steps.title: Pipeline ADD instruction
steps.apply: ADD: IF=1, ID=2, EX=3, WB=4. R2 is produced at end of cycle 3 (forwarding available).
steps.note: ADD is a simple 1-cycle EX instruction.

steps.title: Pipeline MUL instruction with forwarding
steps.apply: MUL: IF=2, ID=3. MUL needs R2 which is ready at end of cycle 3 (from ADD's EX). With forwarding, MUL's EX can start at cycle 4. EX1=4, EX2=5, EX3=6, WB=7. R4 is produced at end of cycle 6.
steps.note: No stall needed for ADD->MUL because forwarding delivers R2 exactly when MUL enters EX.

steps.title: Pipeline SUB instruction with forwarding
steps.apply: SUB: IF=3, ID=4. SUB needs R4 which is ready at end of cycle 6 (from MUL's EX3). SUB's ID completes at cycle 4, but R4 is not available until end of cycle 6, so SUB must stall in ID or wait. SUB's EX starts at cycle 7. EX=7, WB=8.
steps.note: SUB stalls 2 cycles (cycles 5 and 6) waiting for MUL to finish its 3-cycle EX.

steps.title: Count total cycles
steps.apply: Last instruction (SUB) completes WB at cycle 8. Total clock cycles = 8.
steps.note: Without forwarding, there would be more stalls. With forwarding, only the MUL->SUB dependency causes 2 stall cycles.

questions.formula_note: Pipeline execution trace with operand forwarding: result available at end of last EX cycle and forwarded to dependent instruction's EX start. Multi-cycle EX stages cause extra stalls for dependent instructions.

questions.question:
Which of the following are NOT true in a pipelined processor?
I. Bypassing can handle all RAW hazards
II. Register renaming can eliminate all register carried WAR hazards
III. Control hazard penalties can be eliminated by dynamic branch prediction

A. I and II only
B. I and III only
C. II and III only
D. I, II and III
questions.to_find: Which of the given statements about pipelined processors are NOT true

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of pipeline hazard handling techniques. We need to determine which statements are false: (I) whether bypassing/forwarding can eliminate ALL RAW hazards, (II) whether register renaming eliminates ALL WAR hazards, and (III) whether dynamic branch prediction can completely eliminate control hazard penalties.

keywords.explain: A hardware technique that routes a computed result directly from one pipeline stage to another without waiting for WB. It reduces but cannot eliminate all RAW hazards (e.g., load-use still needs 1 stall).
keywords.example: After a LOAD, the data isn't available until the end of MEM stage, so a dependent instruction following immediately still stalls 1 cycle.

keywords.explain: Eliminates false dependencies (WAR and WAW hazards) by mapping architectural registers to a larger set of physical registers.
keywords.example: If R1 is renamed to P5 for a write and the preceding read of R1 still uses the old mapping, there is no hazard.

keywords.explain: Uses runtime history (branch history table, etc.) to predict branch outcomes. Reduces penalties but CANNOT eliminate them - mispredictions still cause penalties.
keywords.example: A 95% accurate predictor still has 5% mispredictions, each costing the branch penalty cycles.

keywords.explain: A hazard where an instruction writes a register that a preceding instruction still needs to read.
keywords.example: I1: READ R1; I2: WRITE R1. If I2 writes before I1 reads, incorrect value is used. Register renaming gives I2 a different physical register.

understand.visual_alt: Analysis of three pipeline hazard-handling techniques and whether they fully eliminate their respective hazards.

given.aim: Identify which statements about hazard-handling techniques are NOT true

terms.example: Counter-example: LOAD followed immediately by a dependent instruction still requires 1 stall (load-use hazard) even with forwarding
terms.connects: Statement I is FALSE because load-use RAW hazards cannot be eliminated by forwarding alone

terms.meaning: Register renaming eliminates ALL register-carried WAR hazards
terms.example: True: renaming gives each write a fresh physical register, so no read can conflict with a later write to the same architectural register
terms.connects: Statement II is TRUE, so it is not the answer

terms.meaning: Dynamic branch prediction can eliminate all control hazard penalties
terms.example: Any prediction scheme has mispredictions; pipeline must be flushed on misprediction, causing penalty cycles
terms.connects: Statement III is FALSE because even the best dynamic predictor cannot guarantee 100% accuracy

given.plan: Evaluate each statement for correctness. Identify the false ones. Statements I and III are false (NOT true), so the answer is 'I and III only'.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I: Bypassing handles all RAW hazards
steps.apply: FALSE. Forwarding/bypassing can handle most RAW hazards by routing results from EX or MEM outputs back to EX inputs. However, it cannot handle the load-use RAW hazard: when a LOAD is immediately followed by an instruction that uses the loaded value, a 1-cycle stall is unavoidable because the data is only available at the end of the MEM stage, after the dependent instruction's EX stage has already started.
steps.note: Load-use hazard is the classic counter-example to 'forwarding handles ALL RAW hazards'.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II: Register renaming eliminates all register-carried WAR hazards
steps.apply: TRUE. WAR (Write After Read) hazards are 'false' or 'name' hazards - they only exist because two instructions share the same architectural register name, not because there is a true data dependency. Register renaming assigns a fresh physical register to each write, so the earlier read still accesses the old physical register while the later write uses a new one. This completely eliminates all WAR hazards.
steps.note: Register renaming also eliminates WAW hazards. It is a complete solution for name hazards.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement III: Control hazard penalties can be eliminated by dynamic branch prediction
steps.apply: FALSE. Dynamic branch prediction (using branch history tables, two-bit predictors, etc.) can significantly reduce control hazard penalties by predicting the branch outcome and speculatively fetching instructions. However, mispredictions still occur and require pipeline flush, incurring penalty cycles. No practical prediction scheme achieves 100% accuracy, so penalties cannot be fully eliminated.
steps.note: Only a scheme that never mispredicts (which is theoretically impossible for all programs) could eliminate all control hazard penalties.

steps.title: Identify statements that are NOT true
steps.apply: Statements I and III are NOT true. Statement II is true. Therefore the answer is 'I and III only'.

solution.result: Statements I and III are NOT true. Answer: B. I and III only.

questions.formula_note: Pipeline hazard handling: forwarding cannot eliminate load-use RAW hazards; register renaming fully eliminates WAR/WAW name hazards; dynamic branch prediction reduces but cannot eliminate all control hazard penalties.

questions.question:
Delayed branching can help in the handling of control hazards. For all delayed conditional branch instructions, irrespective of whether the condition evaluates to true or false:
A. The instruction following the conditional branch instruction in memory is executed
B. The first instruction in the fall through path is executed
C. The first instruction in the taken path is executed
D. The branch takes longer to execute than any other instruction
questions.answer: A. The instruction following the conditional branch instruction in memory is executed
questions.to_find: What happens to the instruction in the delay slot of a delayed conditional branch instruction

understand.plain: Delayed branching is a technique to handle control hazards by defining that the instruction immediately following a branch (in program memory order) is always executed regardless of whether the branch is taken or not. This slot is called the 'branch delay slot'. The compiler fills this slot with a useful instruction (or a NOP if nothing useful can be placed there).

keywords.explain: A branch technique where the instruction immediately following the branch in memory (in the branch delay slot) is always executed before the branch takes effect.
keywords.example: Branch to L1; ADD R1,R2,R3. The ADD always executes, then control goes to L1 or falls through.

keywords.explain: The instruction slot immediately following a branch instruction in memory that is always executed, regardless of branch outcome.
keywords.example: MIPS architecture has a 1-instruction branch delay slot; the instruction in this slot always executes.

keywords.explain: A pipeline hazard caused by branch instructions where the next instruction to fetch is not known until the branch is resolved.
keywords.example: In a 5-stage pipeline, if branch target is computed in EX, 2 instructions are fetched speculatively before the outcome is known.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='480' height='180' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Delayed Branch Execution</text><rect x='20' y='44' width='180' height='32' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>BRANCH (conditional)</text><rect x='20' y='84' width='180' height='32' class='ok'/><text x='110' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>Delay slot instr (ALWAYS runs)</text><text x='215' y='65' class='sub mut'>-- branch resolved here --</text><rect x='20' y='130' width='80' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='149' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Taken path</text><rect x='110' y='130' width='90' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='155' y='149' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Fall-through</text><text x='215' y='149' class='sub mut'>(depends on condition)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory layout showing: conditional branch instruction, then delay slot instruction (always executed), then either taken path or fall-through depending on condition.

given.aim: Identify which instruction is always executed for a delayed conditional branch

terms.meaning: The branch takes effect after the next instruction(s) following it in memory have been executed
terms.example: BRANCH L1; NOP. The NOP always executes, then PC goes to L1 (or PC+8 if not taken).
terms.connects: Defines that the delay slot instruction is always executed

terms.meaning: Whether the branch evaluates to taken or not-taken, the delay slot instruction still executes
terms.example: Even if condition is false and branch falls through, the delay slot instruction still runs
terms.connects: Confirms option A is correct - the instruction in memory following the branch always executes

terms.meaning: The compiler must fill the delay slot with a safe instruction (often from before the branch, or a NOP)
terms.example: Move an instruction from before the branch into the delay slot to keep program semantics correct
terms.connects: Shows why delayed branching is a software-hardware collaboration

given.plan: Recall the definition of delayed branching: the instruction immediately following the branch in memory (the delay slot) is ALWAYS executed regardless of branch outcome. Match this to the given options.

steps.title: Recall the definition of delayed branching
steps.apply: In delayed branching, the processor is designed so that the instruction in the 'delay slot' (the instruction immediately following the branch in sequential memory order) is always fetched and executed before the branch takes effect. This is by architectural definition, not a prediction.
steps.note: This is a hardware/ISA contract - the instruction following the branch in memory always executes.

steps.title: Match with given options
steps.apply: Option A says 'the instruction following the conditional branch instruction in memory is executed' - this is exactly the branch delay slot instruction and it is always executed. Option B (first instruction in fall-through) and C (first instruction in taken path) describe conditional outcomes, not the delay slot. Option D is irrelevant to the question.
steps.note: The key phrase is 'irrespective of whether the condition evaluates to true or false', which matches option A exactly.

solution.result: The instruction following the conditional branch in memory (the branch delay slot instruction) is always executed. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Delayed branching: the instruction immediately after the branch in memory (branch delay slot) is always executed before the branch takes effect, regardless of whether the branch is taken or not taken.
